questions.question_text: Where was Mr. Gessler's shop located?
questions.explanation_text: Mr. Gessler's shop was located in a small by-street in a fashionable part of London.

questions.question_text: Who was Mr. Gessler in the plot, Quality?
questions.explanation_text: Mr. Gessler was a German shoemaker who was settled in London.

questions.question_text: What was the narrator's opinion about Mr. Gessler as a shoemaker?

options.label: A
options.text: He was very impressed with Mr. Gessler

options.label: B
options.text: He found Mr. Gessler to be very ordinary

options.label: C
options.text: He found Mr. Gessler to be pathetic in his work

options.label: D
options.text: He found Mr. Gessler to be not up to the mark

questions.correct_answer: He was very impressed with Mr. Gessler
questions.explanation_text: The author was impressed with Mr. Gessler's skills as a shoemaker. His boots were made of the best quality leathers and they lasted long. The narrator found Mr. Gessler's work to be wonderful and mysterious.

questions.question_text: Why was the author disturbed? (Quality)

options.label: A
options.text: He was worried about his boots

options.label: C
options.text: He was worried about Mr. Gessler’s well being

options.label: D
options.text: He didn’t like the young man

questions.correct_answer: He was worried about Mr. Gessler’s well being
questions.explanation_text: When the author found that the name of the shop was gone. He was worried about Mr. Gessler's well-being.

questions.question_text: What was Mr Gessler's opinion about the large firms?

options.label: A
options.text: They did not hire talented artists like him.

options.label: B
options.text: They succeeded because they advertised a lot.

options.label: C
options.text: They robbed workers like him of their self-respect.

options.label: D
options.text: They earned more by stealing small workers' ideas.

questions.correct_answer: They succeeded because they advertised a lot.
questions.explanation_text: They succeeded because they advertised a lot.

questions.question_text: What material did Mr. Gessler use to make boots?
questions.explanation_text: Mr. Gessler made boots from the finest quality leather. The shoes he manufactured were of superior quality and they were very durable.

questions.question_text: The author was turned out to be: (Quality)
questions.explanation_text: The author was turned out to be a kind person. There are instances in the lesson that shows the author's kindness. The narrator was so deeply moved that he ordered many pairs twice.

questions.question_text: How did the narrator help, Mr. Gessler?

options.label: A
options.text: By ordering several pairs of boots

options.label: B
options.text: By giving him some extra pounds

options.label: C
options.text: By offering him a new job

options.label: D
options.text: By helping him to advertise his products

questions.correct_answer: By ordering several pairs of boots
questions.explanation_text: The narrator was deeply touched on seeing Mr. Gessler struggling hard in this world of cutthroat competition and as a kind gesture, he offered to help him by ordering several pairs of shoes.

questions.question_text: What happened to Mr. Gessler at the end of the plot, Quality?
questions.explanation_text: Mr. Gessler died due to starvation at the end of the story. The narrator visited his shop and was shocked to see that his nameplate was missing and on enquiring, he came to know that Mr. Gessler was no more and somebody had taken over his shop.

questions.question_text: The author's complaint about the boots that creaked made Mr. Gessler:
questions.explanation_text: Once the narrator complained that his last pair of boots creaked. Gessler was shocked to hear it. He agreed to repair or replace them.

questions.question_text: Mr. Gessler's complaint about the big firms was that they:
questions.explanation_text: Mr. Gessler was full of resentment against big firms. He complained about them for spoiling his business. He also spoke about the hard times of his trade. His own business was going down because of the big firms.

questions.question_text: What did Mr. Gessler make in the plot, Quality?
questions.explanation_text: Mr. Gessler used to make boot on orders. He was passionate about his work and believed that shoemaking was art too. He never compromised on the quality of his product.

questions.question_text: What material did Mr. Gessler use to make the boots?
Why did the author visit the shop so infrequently? [Quality]
For how long did the narrator know Mr. Gessler? (Quality)
Where was Gesslers's shop situated?
What proves that Mr. Gessler was not an Englishman?
Where did Mr. Gessler live?
When the author met Mr. Gessler after his brother's death, he found him suddenly very ________.
questions.explanation_text: When the author met Mr. Gessler after his brother's death, he found him suddenly very aged and wan.

questions.question_text: The narrator had known Mr. Gessler for many years for he used to make boots for his ________.
questions.explanation_text: The author of the story, Quality had known the shoemaker for many years for he used to make boots for his father.

questions.question_text: Mr. Gessler lived with his ________.
questions.explanation_text: Mr. Gessler lived with his elder brother in London.

questions.question_text: What was the author's opinion about Mr. Gessler as a bootmaker?
What was Mr. Gessler's complaint against big firms?
Mr. Gessler was spending his days with great difficulty. Give suitable arguments in favour of this. Write your answer in the context of Quality.
How was Mr. Gessler, a failure in business?
What was the effect on Mr. Gessler of the author's remark about a certain pair of boots?
Why did the author order so many pairs of boots? Did he really need them? [Quality]
Do you think Mr. Gessler was a failure as a bootmaker or as a competitive businessman?
What did Mr. Gessler seem to be dreaming about all the time? Why?

Study the following phrases and their meanings. Use them appropriately to complete the sentences that follow.
look after: take care of
look down on: disapprove or regard as inferior
look in (on someone): make a short visit
look into: investigate
look out: be careful
look up: improve
look up to: admire
After a very long spell of heat, the weather is ________ at last.
We have no right to ________ people who do small jobs.
Nitin has always _________ his uncle, who is a self-made man.
The police are ________ the matter thoroughly.
If you want to go out, I will ________ the children for you.
I promise to ________ on your brother when I visit Lucknow next.
_______ when you are crossing the main road.
What were Mr. Gessler's views about big firms?
Mr. Gessler treated boots as though they had a life of their own. Support your answer using evidence from the story.
Why didn't the writer visit Mr Gessler more often?
Why did Mr. Gessler say, "It is an Ardt'?
Mr. Gessler in his last wasn't in good health. Give three examples to prove this. Write your answer in the context of Quality.

Speak to five adults in your neighbourhood. Ask them the following questions (in any language they are comfortable in). Then come back and share your findings with the class.
Do they buy their provisions packed in plastic packets at a big store, or loose, from a smaller store near their house?
Where do they buy their footwear? Do they buy branded footwear, or footwear made locally? What reasons do they have for their preference?
Do they buy ready-made clothes, or buy cloth and get their clothes stitched by a tailor? Which do they think is better?
What is the significance of the title? To whom or to what does it refer?

Based on the following points write a story.
Your aunt has gone to her mother’s house.
Your uncle does his cooking.
He is absent-minded.
He puts vegetables on the stove.
He begins to clean his bicycle outside.
The neighbour calls out saying something is burning.
Your uncle rushes to the kitchen.
To save vegetables, he puts some oil on them.
Unfortunately, it’s machine oil, not cooking oil.
What do you think happens to the vegetables?
Begin like this:
Last month my aunt decided to visit her parents...

Let pairs of students talk to each other about leaving the country. One student repeats Ajit’s statement. The other gives a reason for not agreeing with Ajit. The sentence openings given below should be used.
If I leave this country, I’ll miss...
There are some things which you can get only here, for example...
There are some special days I’ll miss, particularly...
Most of all I’ll miss...because...
I think it’s impossible for me to leave my country because...
How can you leave your own country except when...?
Depends on one’s intention. I can’t leave for good because...
Maybe for a couple of years...

In each of the following words 'ch' represents the same consonant sound as in 'chair'. The words on the left have this sound initially. Those on the right have it finally. Speak each word clearly.


choose
bench


child
march


cheese
peach


chair
wretch


charming
research

feature
archery

matches

Read the following passage carefully and answer the questions:
I knew him from the days of my extreme youth because he made my father's boots. He lived with his elder brother in his shop which was in a small by-street in a fashionable part of London.
The shop had a certain quiet distinction. There was no sign upon it other than the name of Gessler Brothers and in the window a few pairs of boots. He made only what was ordered and what he made never failed to fit. To make boots-such boots as he made-seemed to me then, and still seems to me, mysterious and wonderful.
Name the lesson and its author.
Who is 'he' referred to here?
What was special about the boots he made?
How does the author find his art of making boots?
Find a word similar to "Puzzling" from the passage.

Read the passage and answer the following questions:
But when I come to where his shop has been, his name was gone. I went in very much disturbed. In the shop, there was a young man with an English face.
"Mr. Gessler in?" I said, "No sir", he said. " No, but we can attend to anything with pleasure."
Why did the writer go to that shop?
Who was the young man?
What was wrong in Mr. Gessler's shop?
Why was the writer disturbed?

Read the following passages carefully and answer the questions that follow each of them: (1x5=5)
"Dey get id all," he said , "dey get id by advertisement, nod by work. Dey take id away from us , who lofe our bloods. Id gomes to dis-bresently I haf no work. Every year id gets less. You will see."And looking at his lined face I saw things I had never noticed before, bitter things and bitter struggle and what a lot of grey hairs there seemed suddenly in his red beard!
Name the lesson and its author.
Who does 'dey (they)' refer to?
What does Mr. Gessler complain of?
What do big firms do to make good sale of their products?
Find the word from the above passage that means the same as to marked.

Read the following passage carefully and answer the questions that follow:
One went in, not as into most shop, but restfully, as one enters a church and sitting on the single wooden chair, waited. A guttural sound and the tip-tap of his slippers beating the narrow wooden stairs and he would stand before one without a coat, a little bent, in a leather apron, with sleeves turned back blinking - as if awakened from some dream of boots.
And I would say, "how do you do, Mr. Gessler?" Could you make me a pair of Russian leather boots?"
How was the shop of Mr. Gessler different from the other shops?
How does the author describe Mr. Gessler?
What kind of boots did the author want?
Find from the passage the opposites of:

double
wide
asleep

Read the following passages carefully and answer the questions that follow each of them: (1x5=5)
I knew him from the days of my extreme youth because he made my father's boots. He lived with his elder brother in his shop which was in a small by-street in a fashionable part of London.
The shop had a certain quiet distinction. There was no sign upon it other than the name of Gessler Brothers and in the window a few pairs of boots. He made only what was ordered, and what he made never failed to fit. To make boots- such boots as he made- seemed to me then, and still seems to me, mysterious and wonderful.
Where was Gessler's shop located?
What was distinct about Gessler's shop ?
What does the author admire the most about Gessler brothers' mode of work?
With whom did Gessler work in his shop?
Find the word from the above passage that means opposite to succeed.

Read the following passages carefully and answer the questions that follow each of them: (1x5=5)
"Ah!" he said, poor old man starved himself. Slow starvation, the doctor called it! You see he went to work in such a way! Would keep the shop on; wouldn't have a soul touch his boots except himself. When he got an order, it took him such a time. People won't wait. He lost everybody. And there he'd sit, going on and on. I will say that for him—not a man in London made a better boot. But look at the competition! He never advertised! Would have the best leather too, and do it all himself. Well, there it is. What could you expect with his ideas? But starvation!
Who does the phrase poor old man refer to?
What did he die of?
Why did he lose his customers?
What made his condition miserable?
Find the word from the above passage that means opposite to well-fed.

Read the following passages carefully and answer the questions that follow each of them: (1x5=5)
One went in, not as into most shop, but restfully, as one enters a church, and sitting on the single wooden chair, waited. A guttural sound and the tip-tap of his slippers beating the narrow wooden stairs and he would stand before one without a coat, a little bent, in a leather apron, with sleeves turned back blinking-as if awakened from some dream of boots.
And I would say, "How do you do, Mr. Gessler?" Could you make me a pair of Russian-leather boots?
How did the author feel as he entered Gessler's shop? Why?
How does the author describe Mr. Gessler?
What order did he place with him?
Find the word from the above passage that means opposite to asleep.
Find the word from the above passage that means same as garment.

Read the extract given below and answer the following questions:
But his face and voice made so deep an impression that during the next few minutes I ordered many pairs. They lasted longer than ever. And I was not able to go to him for nearly 2 years.
It was many months before my next visit to his shop.
Name the speaker in the above lines.
Whom does his refer to in the above lines?
Why his voice and face made such an impact?
What has caused such a reaction from him?

Read the passage and answer the following questions:
I knew him from the days of my extreme youth because he made my father's boots. He lived with his elder brother in his shop which was in a small by-street in a fashionable part of London.
The shop had a certain quiet distinction. There was no sign upon it other than the name of Gessler Brothers and in the window a few pair of boots.
In what sense did Gessler's shop have a certain distinction?
What does the author admire most about Gessler Brothers' mode of work?
With whom did Gessler work in his shop?
Find the word from the above passage that means the opposite of 'common'.
